name: Shipping / Tanker Rates
summary: Day 52 Sunday. IRGC KINETIC BROADENED FROM TANKER-ONLY TO MULTI-TYPE WITHIN 24 HOURS — TANKER + CONTAINER + CRUISE-SHIP THREAT. Saturday April 18 kinetic widened beyond initial VLCC fire on Jag Arnav / Sanmar Herald: (a) container ship damaged by 'unknown projectile' (rocket/missile/drone — standoff munition, distinct from small-arms gunfire); (b) Malta-flagged cruise liner Mein Schiff 4 master-logged IRGC VHF threat to 'fire at and destroy the ship' plus nearby splash off Oman coast. Sunday intel validates Frontline +5.6% Friday ($37.13) as counter-directional physical-market signal: VLCC TD3C ~WS 513 (moderated from WS 600+ peak) with ~$130-150K/day earnings. Monday AWRP re-hike to 1.2-1.8% likely — insurers did NOT step down on Saturday kinetic yet; Monday is first re-underwriting window. Frontline +5.6% Friday is now validated by Sunday intel (broadening kinetic + institutional SNSC consolidation). Shipping-tankers → strait-of-hormuz edge remains REINFORCED AS INDEPENDENT SIGNAL. Kpler: 8 vessels transited Hormuz via IRGC-designated routes Friday (~10-15% of pre-war 120/day). Physical-futures gap widens to $40 — tanker equity expression of Dated Brent $132 view holds. Dual Russian (April 11) + Iranian (April 19/20) sanctions waivers expiring remove ~90+ vessels from compliant trade = structural fleet-tightness shock orthogonal to deal optimism.

signals.content: VLCC rates hit all-time high of $423,736/day on Baltic Exchange (TD3C MEG-China), up 94% in single day — highest in nearly 50-year history of the index. War risk insurance being cancelled by major providers.
VLCC Kalamos set individual vessel record at $770,000/day, loading at Yanbu for India's west coast. Normal rates: $80,000-100,000/day. Current rates imply freight cost adds $7/bbl to oil.
Cape of Good Hope crossings increased 89%. Ships rerouting around Africa adding 10-14 days to transit. CMA CGM introduced $3,000/FEU emergency surcharge.
150 ships stranded around Strait of Hormuz. Insurance companies cancelling war risk coverage for vessels in Gulf.

Marine war risk insurance premiums up 1,000%+ — hull war risk premium now ~$7.5 million per trip (up from ~$625,000 pre-conflict). Rate at 3% of hull value, up from 0.2%. Lloyd's List reports Gulf premiums 'topping double-digit millions per trip.' Many insurers stopped covering Hormuz transit entirely, making strait 'de facto closed' for commercial vessels without government backstop. US government created $20 billion maritime reinsurance facility via DFC with Chubb as lead underwriter to keep shipping moving. Container freight: Drewry World Container Index at $2,172/40ft (Mar 19), up third consecutive week. CMA CGM raised surcharge to $265/TEU. Iran operating selective blockade — only 16 AIS-visible crossings in most recent 7-day window. Frontline (FRO) +2.1% to $34.55 as tanker demand remains extreme.

VLCC rates at 50-year all-time highs: $401K-$445K/day on Baltic Exchange (TD3C), with some fixtures at $538K-$770K/day. War risk insurance at 1-7.5% of hull value ($2-15M extra per VLCC transit), up from 0.25% pre-war. Some insurers suspended Gulf coverage entirely. Market bifurcated: Western operators (Frontline +0.2% despite record rates) locked out by insurance costs, while IRGC-tolled yuan passages serve Chinese/non-Western vessels at $2M/transit. Container freight: ~$2,200/FEU global, $4,000+ Shanghai-Dubai (+110%). Emergency surcharges on Gulf routes.

Kuwaiti tanker Al-Salmi attacked by Iranian drone at Dubai Port overnight (March 31, ~12:10 AM). Fire extinguished by 4:30 AM. All 24 crew safe. No oil leakage. One of the most significant maritime attacks of the war — attacking a neutral country's vessel in UAE territorial waters. Undermines 'selective Hormuz opening' narrative: Iran simultaneously allowing some ships through while attacking others at port. Insurance implications severe — if vessels not safe at port, war risk premiums for entire Gulf reprice higher. Iran has NOT claimed or denied. Also: Houthi still zero confirmed Red Sea attacks (Day 32) — deliberately preserving shipping card as leverage. Houthis told Lloyd's List 'no reason to prevent' Yanbu VLCC trade 'at present' — revealing deliberate restraint. Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted or suspended Red Sea/Suez. Bab al-Mandeb at ~57 transits/24hr vs normal. CONFIRMED (multiple independent sources).

Frontline +4.4% DESPITE oil pullback — the structural tanker story. VLCC day rates at historic records: $424K-$758K/day (Clarksons, Splash247). Adamantios at world-record $537,913/day to Reliance. Kalamos at $758K/day to BPCL. War risk insurance premiums 0.25% → up to 3% of vessel value ($6M+ per Hormuz transit). Ships rerouting around Africa → longer voyage = fewer available vessels = rate surge. Tanker rates driven by voyage DISTANCE and vessel AVAILABILITY, not oil price direction. Even with oil -6.6%, rerouting persists regardless of peace rhetoric. Insurance premiums take months to normalize. Most INELASTIC crisis beneficiary. Day 33 zero Houthi Red Sea shipping attacks — commercial closure via carrier withdrawal (Maersk, CMA CGM, Hapag-Lloyd, MSC rerouted), not military blockade. Al-Salmi VLCC attacked at Dubai port anchorage — insurance must reprice for entire Gulf, not just Hormuz. (Sources: Clarksons, Splash247, Maritime Executive, Bloomberg, UKMTO)

CONFIRMED: Iran struck QatarEnergy-chartered tanker AQUA 1 near Ras Laffan (17nm north) with 3 cruise missiles — 2 intercepted, 1 hit. 21 crew safe. IRGC claimed responsibility, alleged Israeli ties. Iran reportedly gave Qatar advance notice — calculated escalation, not indiscriminate. Second vessel attack in 2 days (after Al-Salmi VLCC at Dubai port). Attack pattern expanding from Hormuz chokepoint to Gulf-wide — Dubai port, then Qatar waters. (Sources: QatarEnergy, Al Jazeera, AGBI)

CONFIRMED: VLCC rates remain in 'never ever before' territory. Peak $601,569/day; now $112-117K/day — still 3-5x pre-crisis levels. Lloyd's List notes rates partly 'imaginary' — illiquid market, panic fixing. Key structural signal: Frontline tanker stock -0.1% FLAT despite Brent crude -10.5% — shipping disruption is more entrenched than the financial oil premium. Tanker rates driven by voyage distance and vessel availability, not oil price. (Sources: Lloyd's List, Baltic Exchange, yfinance)

CONFIRMED: Container freight (Drewry WCI) at $2,279/FEU, +5% WoW — 4th consecutive weekly increase. Shanghai-Jebel Ali spiked from $1,800 to $4,000+. Surcharges $800-$4,000/container across Gulf routes. Container shipping disruption widening beyond tanker market. (Sources: Drewry)

Frontline tankers +5.1% to $36.60 — shipping stocks rallying on war continuation signal. VLCC rates at $112-117K/day (down from $601K peak but still 3-5x pre-crisis). AQUA 1 tanker (QatarEnergy-chartered) struck near Ras Laffan April 1. Al-Salmi VLCC hit at Dubai anchorage March 31. Two shipping attacks in 2 days — both in Gulf ally waters, not Hormuz. War risk insurance: 1%+ hull value ($10-14M/voyage). Container freight WCI: $2,279/FEU (+5% WoW). Maersk/CMA CGM/Hapag-Lloyd rerouting to Cape. (Sources: yfinance, Lloyd's List, Baltic Exchange, Al Jazeera)

Frontline +5.1% to $36.60. VLCC rates $45,941-55,010/day (ME-China) — down from $445K peak but still 3-5x pre-crisis. War risk insurance 1%+ hull value ($10-14M/voyage), 5x pre-crisis. Lloyd's JWC expanded to Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, Oman. Container freight rising: Asia-Med +15% WoW to $3,850/FEU. BOAT ETF +1.4%. (Sources: Baltic Exchange, Lloyd's List, Freightos, yfinance)

NEW: US DFC + Chubb announced $40B marine reinsurance facility ($20B DFC-backed, $20B from Chubb/AIG/Berkshire/CNA/Liberty/Starr/Travelers). US government backstopping Gulf shipping insurance. This reduces 'invisible siege' mechanism — if commercial insurance won't cover transits, government will. Net effect: some tankers may resume Gulf transit, marginally easing supply constraint. BUT physical risk remains — insurance doesn't stop missiles. Separately, Hezbollah CLAIMS (unverified) cruise missile hit on Israeli warship 68nm off Lebanese coast — if confirmed, represents naval threat escalation for all shipping. Container freight rising: Asia-US West $2,127/FEU (+8% WoW), Asia-Med $3,850/FEU (+15% WoW), Asia-US East $3,069/FEU. 20,000 seafarers stranded. Day 38 zero Houthi Red Sea attacks.

Hormuz weekly transits hit war-high: 21 ships over April 5-6 weekend (Bloomberg Tracker). Yanbu congestion worsening: 32 supertankers stacked (up from 27). March handled 47 VLCC loadings — 4x pre-war monthly average of 11-12. Port throughput ~4M bpd vs 7M bpd pipeline delivery — port is binding constraint. Saudi informed customers April allocations reduced. VLCC day rates moderated from March peak $445K/day to ~$55K/day MEG-China — chaos premium absorbed, stabilizing at elevated level. Frontline -0.3% despite oil +3.1% — structural repricing embedded (+59% 3M), daily moves now stable. Marine war risk insurance still 1.5-3.0% of hull value (US/UK/Israeli ships at 3%), $10-14M per VLCC trip. Container freight: Asia-Med +15%, Asia-USWC +8%. (Sources: Bloomberg Hormuz Tracker, House of Saud, Fortune, Seatrade Maritime, Freightos, Lloyd's List)

VLCC ME-China rates $200K-400K/day spot range (still 3-10x pre-crisis); peak $424K hit March 3. Down from peak; still extreme. Hormuz transits at WAR HIGHS per Bloomberg Tracker — but selectively under Iran's three-tier system, NOT a free reopening. War risk insurance $10-14M/voyage; 1%+ hull value; Hapag-Lloyd $3,500/container surcharge. Lloyd's: 'Safety not insurance' is what's stopping ships. 88% of Lloyd's market still underwriting at extreme premiums. The bifurcation: Iran-friendly flags transit; Western/Israeli flags do not. Frontline (FRO) $36.48 / -0.3% 1D / +7.8% 1M / +59.0% 3M — mild giveback off $36.60.

EVENING UPDATE: VLCC rates moderated from $424K/day peak to ~$112K/day (WS126.5) — NOT because risk decreased but because almost no one is transiting the Gulf. Lloyd's List: ME VLCC rates 'imaginary' due to so few fixtures. Frontline -0.3% — paradox: tanker stocks should rally on disruption but revenue depends on voyage count. Container freight $2,287/FEU (flat w/w, +29-31% since Feb). War risk surcharges $1,500-4,000/container. Bunker costs doubled. US government doubled Hormuz reinsurance from $20B to $40B DFC (April 3). (Sources: Baltic Exchange, Lloyd's List, Drewry, S&P Global)

WAR RISK INSURANCE UNCHANGED ON CEASEFIRE — LLOYD'S: 'PHYSICAL NORMALISATION NOT COMPRESSED BY POLITICAL WILL' [CONFIRMED]. Despite Brent crashing -13.5% on the ceasefire announcement, Lloyd's List notes that hull war cover for Hormuz transit remains at $10-14M per voyage (1% of hull value, ~30,000x pre-crisis pricing). Lloyd's: 'Even if a ceasefire takes hold, reinsurers must rebuild risk models from scratch. Each vessel seeking Gulf transit must be individually re-underwritten on a voyage-by-voyage basis. Pricing must clear across the entire reinsurance chain, from treaty reinsurer to P&I club to shipowner. Capacity that was withdrawn at the treaty level must be reinstated through negotiation, not announcement. This is a sequential, multi-party process that cannot be compressed by political will, executive order, or diplomatic breakthrough.' Implication: physical Hormuz flow restoration takes WEEKS even on a holding ceasefire. The Brent -13.5% move prices a faster restoration than is operationally possible. ~40 VLCCs remain anchored 5-day waits at Yanbu. Frontline (FRO) 1Y charter rates at $76,900/day vs spot at AG-Far East ~$112K/day TCE (down from $445K mid-March peak — TRAFFIC stopped, not risk reduced).

War risk insurance rates dipped post-ceasefire to 0.35-0.45% of vessel value (from peak 3.5-10%), but remain 7-10x pre-war. Neil Roberts (LMA): 'It is highly unlikely that trade into the Gulf will simply resume.' Reinsurers exercised 72-hour cancellation clauses at war start — restoring takes weeks. Lloyd's Market Association: 88% of participants still have appetite but at crisis premiums. Andrew James (Arthur J. Gallagher): underwriters 'already recognizing the ceasefire and reducing rates for some risks.' (Source: TT News, CBS News, LMA, S&P Global)

Frontline (FRO) +3.3% to $36.15 ON oil crash — ANOMALY. Tanker stock UP despite Brent -11.5%. Market pricing physical backlog clearing: 40+ VLCCs backlogged at Yanbu, ceasefire → eventual Hormuz reopening → more shipping demand. War risk premiums ($6-14M/trip) persist → tanker companies paid premium rates. Physical market at $120+ = high-value cargo = high freight revenue per barrel. Bellwether signal: Frontline priced PHYSICAL reality ($120+ cargo), not PAPER reality ($96 futures). (Source: yfinance, market data)

479 vessels remain stranded near Hormuz as of April 9 per MarineTraffic: 426 tankers, 34 LPG carriers, 19 LNG ships. Only 3-4 ships transited April 8-9 — first were bulk carriers, NOT oil tankers. Lars Jensen (Vespucci Maritime): 'Vessels likely to exit the Persian Gulf but more cautious entering — owners don't want to risk being trapped again.' CBS News: even if ceasefire holds perfectly, oil exports return to pre-war levels by July 2026 at earliest. (Source: Bloomberg, MarineTraffic, 19FortyFive, CBS News)

Day 43. CEASEFIRE FAILED SHIPPING TEST. Three Chinese VLCCs (Cospearl Lake, He Rong Hai, Yuan Hua Hu) halted at Hormuz entrance — NOT through. IRGC toll system intact. S&P Global: ~9 vessels transited April 8-10 combined vs 100+/day pre-war = ~3% capacity. Stranded vessel count revised SHARPLY UP: Bloomberg 800+, IMO 2,000, Press TV/Global Security 3,000. 20,000+ seafarers trapped. War risk insurance at 1.5-3.0% hull value (down from 3.5-10% peak). LMA Roberts: 'safety, not insurance' is the constraint — even free coverage wouldn't restart traffic. VLCC rates ~$400-424K/day (down from $423K peak). Frontline -3.7% on ceasefire unwind hope but physical reality unchanged. Lars Jensen (Vespucci): 'even best case, 3-6 months to normalize.' (Source: Bloomberg AIS, NBC News, CNBC, S&P Global, Lloyd's List, Insurance Journal)

Day 44. Hormuz traffic DECLINING post-ceasefire: 11 ships Apr 8 → 5 Apr 9 → 6-7 Apr 10. Three Chinese VLCCs still NOT through. VLCC rates moderated to ~$45,941/day MEG-China (from $206K peak Feb, brief spike to $48,100 April 7). War risk insurance DOWN to 0.35-0.45% hull (from 0.8-1.5% peak) — ceasefire partially pricing but still 40-80% above pre-war 0.25%. US doubled reinsurance to $40B. Container freight Asia-USWC ~$2,400/FEU (+11% WoW, +40% since pre-war). BELLWETHER: Maersk completed FIRST Red Sea transit in two years — strongest signal of bifurcation durability. Houthi Day 44 zero attacks. Windward: ~3,200 vessels stranded. Frontline -1.4%. Russian-flagged VLCC MT Arhimeda transited INTO the Gulf Apr 9-10 heading toward Kharg Island — Russia testing inbound passage. ADNOC CEO Al Jaber: 'The Strait of Hormuz is not open. That is not freedom of navigation. That is coercion.'

US Navy mine clearing operation launched April 11 could create an alternative corridor to IRGC toll system — the most significant Hormuz shipping development since the toll was established March 27. Admiral Cooper: 'establishing a new passage, will share safe pathway with maritime industry.' If successful, commercial vessels could transit without Iranian escort or toll payment, structurally undermining IRGC's selective control mechanism. VLCC rates moderated to ~$151K/day TCE (WS163) from $423K peak — ceasefire and IRGC toll system dampening rates, still 3-4x pre-crisis ~$40-50K/day. War risk insurance at 0.35-0.45% hull (non-transit), 1.5-3% hull (transit) — down from peaks but still elevated. Container freight Asia-USWC ~$2,400/FEU (+11% WoW, +40% since pre-war). Islamabad talks failure means diplomatic path to Hormuz reopening weakened; physical disruption persists.

AFTERNOON UPDATE: McNally (Rapidan Energy Group) assessed mine clearing operation is Phase 1 of broader US strategy to degrade Iran's Hormuz interdiction. With underwater drones and littoral combat ships arriving in days, US could reduce Iran's maritime control to "manageable levels" -- meaning insurance and escort operations for commercial shipping could resume by late April. FALSIFIABLE: If correct, VLCC rate moderation and war risk insurance quotes should decline within 10-14 days as escort capabilities materialize. If wrong, IRGC escalation (mines relaid, fast boat attacks) forces US to choose between naval combat and withdrawal. VLCC rates at ~WS163 / ~K/day TCE (down from WS473/K peak). War risk insurance 0.35-0.45% hull (non-transit), 1.5-3.0% (Hormuz transit). Container freight: Shanghai-LA +9% WoW, Rotterdam-NY +25% WoW. Maersk seeking /TEU emergency bunker surcharge. Only ~3% normal Hormuz traffic -- rates "unreliable" per Clarksons. (Sources: Fortune/McNally, CENTCOM, DefenseScoop, Lloyd's List, Drewry, Freightos)

Blockade fundamentally reshaping shipping dynamics. VLCC rates ~$151K/day (down from $423K peak, still 3-4x pre-crisis). Over 150 tankers anchored outside Hormuz. Only ~3% of pre-war traffic transiting. War risk insurance expected to re-spike from ceasefire rates (0.35-0.45%) toward 1.5-3% on Islamabad failure. Container freight: Asia-USWC +11% WoW to ~$2,400/FEU. Maersk structural return to Red Sea via MECL service — first top-5 carrier re-entry since 2023.

BLOCKADE DAY 1 OPERATIONAL DATA [WITH EVENING CORRECTIONS]. 31 vessels transited Hormuz in 24 hours with 6 carrying cargo — a fraction of normal ~80-100/day but marginally higher than near-zero expected. Comoros-registered sanctioned tanker M/T Elpis (Iran shadow fleet) successfully transited AFTER blockade took effect — US Navy did NOT intercept, testing blockade credibility. M/T Ostria (Botswana-flagged, landlocked = false registry) changed destination 41 min after deadline. EVENING CORRECTION: M/T Rich Starry (Malawi-flagged, Chinese-owned, ~250K bbl methanol from Hamriyah) initially reversed course off Qeshm Island but made a SECOND ATTEMPT in the evening and appears to have transited through the Strait by early Tuesday UTC. Al Jazeera: 'at least three vessels, including two US-sanctioned tankers' entered the Gulf on Day 1. Combined with Elpis, 3+ sanctioned vessels transited — blockade credibility significantly weaker than morning assessed. US is NOT interdicting sanctioned/shadow-fleet vessels, only deterring legitimate commercial shipping. Perverse outcome: Iran's illicit oil network continues while legitimate trade is strangled. Over 150 tankers anchored outside strait (source: Bloomberg, CBS News, MarineTraffic, Fortune). EVENING CORRECTION ON INSURANCE: WAR RISK INSURANCE IS NOT SUSPENDED — Lloyd's List clarifies P&I clubs have NOT cancelled war risk cover. Coverage available but at prohibitive prices: 2.5-5% hull value ($10-14M per VLCC). LMA: 88% of Lloyd's marine war market has appetite to underwrite. Constraint is PRICE not AVAILABILITY. Blockade re-spiked premiums above pre-ceasefire levels. Kennedys Law: 'reshaped marine insurance risk landscape.' US DFC $40B reinsurance facility attempting to fill gap. Frontline +13.2% 1M, +37.7% 3M. VLCC TD3C ~$55,010/day. BOAT ETF +7.1% 1M. (Sources: Bloomberg, CBS News, MarineTraffic, Fortune, Lloyd's List, CNBC, Kennedys Law, Caixin, Al Jazeera, Nikkei Asia)

Blockade Day 2-3: CENTCOM claims 'completely halted' — DISPUTED. 3+ sanctioned vessels transited Day 1 per Kpler/Al Jazeera. US NOT interdicting shadow fleet. War risk insurance EASING: 0.8-1.0% hull value (from 2.5% peak), still 4-6x pre-crisis levels (0.15-0.25%). LMA: 88% of Lloyd's war market has appetite to underwrite; 'safety concerns, not insurance availability' driving reduced traffic. Market pricing de-escalation: Frontline -2.0% to $34.16, BOAT ETF -0.9% to $40.94, XLE -2.0%. Energy complex selling on 'deal within days' expectations, not physical reality. VLCC rates ~$55,010/day (stabilized from $206K peak). Container freight: Asia-USWC +11% to ~$2,400/FEU (+40% since pre-war). Over 150 tankers anchored outside Hormuz. (Sources: CENTCOM, Al Jazeera/Kpler, Lloyd's List, LMA, Baltic Exchange/Seatrade, Freightos, yfinance)

IRGC KINETIC BROADENED TO MULTI-TYPE WITHIN 24 HOURS — TANKER + CONTAINER + CRUISE-SHIP THREAT [CONFIRMED multi-source; Mein Schiff VHF is master-report]. Saturday April 18 kinetic broadened beyond initial tanker-only frame: (1) VLCC Jag Arnav (Indian-flagged, ~2M bbl Iraqi crude) fired on by IRGC fast-boats; Sanmar Herald nearby bridge-window damage. (2) Container ship damaged by 'unknown projectile' in same general Strait approach — visible container damage reported by vessel master (Military Times, Al Jazeera citing UKMTO). A projectile (rocket/missile/drone) is OPERATIONALLY DISTINCT from gun-fire: widens escalation ladder from warning-shot small arms to standoff munitions. (3) Malta-flagged cruise liner Mein Schiff 4 master-logged IRGC VHF threat to 'fire at and destroy the ship' plus nearby splash off Oman coast (UKMTO advisory traffic, IBTimes Australia). Passenger-vessel threat expands target set from commercial cargo to passenger shipping — if escalated to actual hit, triggers IMO-level maritime emergency rather than purely commercial insurance response. Three confirmed vessel-events within 24 hours (tanker-fire, container-projectile, cruise-threat) = broadening sweep, not single warning shot. Frontline +5.6% Friday ($37.13) now validated as counter-directional on Hormuz-open day. VLCC TD3C at ~WS 513 (moderated from WS 600+ peak) with ~$130-150K/day earnings — elevated but off peaks. Monday AWRP re-hike to 1.2-1.8% likely; insurers did NOT step down on Saturday kinetic yet. (Sources: UKMTO advisory traffic, Military Times, Al Jazeera, Baltic Exchange, Clarksons, Lloyd's List, IBTimes Australia, Seatrade)

FRONTLINE +5.6% TO $37.13 ON HORMUZ-OPEN DAY — THE CLEANEST EQUITY-MARKET WINDOW INTO PHYSICAL GULF DISRUPTION [CONFIRMED]. On the session that crashed Brent -7.6%, WTI -11.3%, CF Industries -9.6%, and Cheniere -4.4%, Frontline rallied +5.6% to $37.13 (+15.5% 1M, +47.8% 3M). BOAT ETF $41.33 (+1.2%). Why tankers RISE on a day everything else in energy complex crashes: (1) VLCC TD3C $130K/day (~WS 78) still 2.5-3x historical $50K — rates sticky, spot market thin, war-risk surcharges embedded — rates take WEEKS to compress even after news catalysts; (2) Dual Russian (April 11) + Iranian (April 19) sanctions waivers expiring remove ~90+ vessels from compliant trade = structural fleet-tightness shock orthogonal to deal optimism; (3) Earnings expectations up — Q1 included late-March rate spike; Q2 likely retains $100-130K/day average even post-Araghchi; (4) Insurance costs passed through — AWRP ~1% hull unchanged — Frontline margins WIDEN during war-risk periods. Graph Engineer reassessment: shipping-tankers → strait-of-hormuz edge is REINFORCED AS INDEPENDENT SIGNAL. It held as deal-rhetoric orthogonal while fertilizer-urea edge co-moved (CF -9.6% refuted the 'independent of deal pricing' framing of that edge). Physical sense: tanker rates set in real spot market with limited fleet supply; fertilizer futures set by expectations of supply arriving months out. Kpler: 8 vessels transited Hormuz via IRGC-designated routes Friday (~10-15% of 120/day pre-war); oil-tanker traffic near-zero. CENTCOM: 21 vessels turned back since April 13. Reversibility: Frontline holds +5% gain through Monday Islamabad outcome — rate structure only normalizes if BOTH waivers renewed (not happening) OR Iran+Russia return to compliant markets (requires signed deal + year+ implementation). Neither is a near-term catalyst. (Sources: yfinance FRO/BOAT, Baltic Exchange/Clarksons, Lloyd's List/Strauss Center, Kpler, CENTCOM)

edges.context: Day 52 Sunday. IRGC kinetic broadened to multi-type (tanker + container-projectile + cruise-VHF threat) within 24 hours. VLCC TD3C ~WS 513 (~$130-150K/day). Frontline +5.6% Friday validated by Sunday intel as counter-directional physical-market signal. AWRP ~1% hull did NOT step back up on Saturday kinetic yet — Monday re-hike to 1.2-1.8% likely. Dual Russian+Iranian sanctions remove ~90 vessels. Edge REINFORCED AS INDEPENDENT SIGNAL.

Day 51. Frontline +5.6% to $37.13 on Brent -7.6% day — cleanest counter-directional signal. Tanker equity pricing physical reality ($132 Dated Brent view) while futures pricing $91 deal path. Physical-futures gap $40 widest of war. War-risk insurance unchanged; fleet tightness structural via dual sanctions.
India's imports face longer routes and much higher shipping costs on top of higher commodity prices.

Day 34 zero confirmed Houthi Red Sea shipping attacks. Bab al-Mandeb in commercial closure via carrier withdrawal. Bloomberg: Iran pressing Houthis to resume but 'divisions within leadership.' Houthis told Lloyd's List 'no reason to prevent' Yanbu trade 'at present' — preserving shipping card as leverage. Washington and Riyadh told European allies they believe Houthis 'currently seek to avoid further escalation.'

trigger_points.condition: Houthis begin confirmed Red Sea attacks (verified by maritime trackers)
trigger_points.mechanism: Double chokepoint: Hormuz AND Red Sea blocked. Only Cape of Good Hope remains. Shipping rates would spike further from already-historic levels. As of Day 33, Houthi threats remain rhetoric only — zero attacks confirmed.
